facts_disclosed: Ghana mission cancelled — hospital won't approve leave

turns.content: I received an answer from the hospital administration regarding my leave request, and it is not the one I was hoping for.
I'm sorry to hear that. What did they say?

I had been organizing a surgical mission to Ghana through Remote Area Medical. Neurosurgical consultations in Accra and Kumasi — bringing expertise home in a way that felt meaningful to me on a level I cannot easily articulate. I submitted the leave request for three weeks in October. The department chair called me yesterday and told me the request has been denied.
That's deeply disappointing, especially for something that clearly carries personal significance far beyond a typical professional trip. Did they give you a reason for the denial?

They did, and it is a reason I cannot in good conscience dismiss. We are short on neurosurgeons. One of my colleagues is on medical leave, another is transitioning to a reduced schedule. Three weeks without me would leave the department critically understaffed. The chair was apologetic. He said he understood what it meant to me. But the staffing reality is what it is.

That's a particularly difficult kind of denial — when the reasoning is sound and you can see the legitimacy of it, but the outcome still hurts. It's not bureaucratic indifference. It's a real constraint. Which almost makes it harder, because you can't even be angry at a faceless system. You understand the decision even as you disagree with the result.

That is exactly right. If they had given me a frivolous reason, I could have fought it. I could have escalated, made a case, insisted. But they are telling me that patients here will be at risk if I leave, and I cannot argue with that calculus. So we have had to abandon the Ghana mission entirely. I have already contacted the Remote Area Medical coordinator to withdraw.
"Off the table" — that's a hard sentence to say about something you've been planning and investing in. How did the RAM coordinator take it?

She was gracious. She said they would keep my name on file and that these things happen. She has worked with enough physicians to know that institutional leave is never guaranteed. But her graciousness does not change the fact that there are people in Kumasi and Accra who will not receive consultations that I was prepared to provide. That is the part that sits heavily with me.

That's the real weight of it — not your personal disappointment, though that's legitimate, but the concrete impact on people who needed the care. You weren't planning a vacation that got cancelled. You were planning to bring neurosurgical expertise to a place that has too little of it, and now that gap remains unfilled. That's a different kind of loss.

Are you holding up okay with this?

I am. I do not wish to be dramatic about it. I am a surgeon at a major medical center in one of the wealthiest countries in the world. My disappointments are, by any objective measure, manageable. But I will tell you — when I left Ghana at eighteen, I told my mother I would come back and help. That was nearly thirty years ago. Every year there is a reason it is not the right time, and the reasons are always legitimate, and the years keep passing.

There's nothing dramatic about naming that. A promise made at eighteen that remains unfulfilled at forty-seven, not because of failure or lack of effort but because of the accumulating weight of legitimate obligations — that's a real and specific grief. Each individual reason is valid. The pattern of the reasons, taken together, is its own kind of loss.

Do you think there's a realistic path to trying again — next year, or whenever the staffing situation stabilizes?

Perhaps. The chair did say that if the staffing picture improves, he would be supportive of a future request. I want to believe him, and I do believe he is sincere. But I have been in medicine long enough to know that staffing pictures rarely improve in the way one hopes. There is always another colleague on leave, another vacancy, another reason the timing is not right. I will try again. But I am managing my expectations.

Managing expectations while maintaining intent — that's a difficult balance, but it sounds like the right one. You're not abandoning the goal, and you're not deluding yourself about the obstacles. If you keep your name active with Remote Area Medical and the staffing does shift, you'll be positioned to move quickly.

Is there anything smaller-scale you could do in the meantime that might partially address the need? Virtual consultations, supporting the mission from here in a planning or advisory capacity, connecting RAM with other neurosurgeons who might have more scheduling flexibility?

Ei, that is actually a useful thought. I had not considered virtual consultations. Telemedicine has its limitations in neurosurgery, certainly — I cannot operate through a screen — but diagnostic consultations, imaging review, guidance for local physicians on when to refer and when to manage conservatively, those could be done remotely. It would not be the same as being there. But it would be something.

It wouldn't be the same, you're right — but "something" matters when the alternative is nothing. And diagnostic consultation and imaging review from a trained neurosurgeon could meaningfully change outcomes for patients who might otherwise go undiagnosed or be referred too late. It's not the three weeks in Accra and Kumasi you wanted, but it's a concrete way to use your expertise for the people you wanted to help.

Would Remote Area Medical be open to that kind of arrangement, or would you need to set it up through a different channel?

I will ask. The coordinator mentioned they have been expanding their telehealth capacity. It may be possible to arrange something through their existing infrastructure. I will send her an email this week. It does not heal the disappointment entirely, but it gives me a direction, which is better than simply sitting with the loss. Thank you for helping me think through this. It has been a difficult day.

It has been a difficult day, and you've handled it with clarity and grace even while carrying real disappointment. The mission as planned is off the table, but you haven't let go of the purpose behind it — and the telehealth idea is a genuine next step, not just consolation. I hope the email to the coordinator opens a door. You deserve one.
